serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled zucchini is approximately one cup, weighing around 120 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Zucchini, boiled — Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for 'Zucchini, boiled' (cr_id F14040034). Core macros, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are exact matches. Saturated fat value of 0.1g per 100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for zucchini boiled (NDB 11478) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately; this is documented in notes. Additional micronutrients (vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11478) as a secondary approved source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for boiled zucchini exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated from the closest analogous entry for summer squash / zucchini (boiled) reported in Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, a peer-reviewed journal. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (15) × carbohydrate per serving (2.0g × 1.2 serving factor = 2.4g) / 100 = 0.36, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1 mg/100g) and meaningful potassium (201 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Boiled zucchini is an excellent DASH-diet-compatible vegetable for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium content and potassium of 201 mg per 100g support favourable sodium-to-potassium balance and blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2g/100g), low GI (~15), and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2g of carbohydrates per 100g, a GI of approximately 15, and a glycemic load of 0.4 per serving, boiled zucchini has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is highly suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (0.4g), negligible saturated fat (0.1g), zero cholesterol, and dietary fibre (1.3g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled zucchini supports cardiovascular health through its very low saturated fat and cholesterol content, combined with dietary fibre and potassium that contribute to healthy lipid profiles and blood pressure management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled zucchini is a simple, minimally processed preparation of green zucchini (Cucurbita pepo), a summer squash originating from the Mediterranean region, cooked in water until tender. The result is a pale green vegetable with a soft, yielding texture and a mild, slightly sweet flavour. Nutritionally, boiled zucchini is extremely low in calories (18 kcal/100g), carbohydrates (2g), and fat (0.4g), making it one of the most diet-friendly vegetables available. It provides dietary fibre (1.3g), potassium (201mg), calcium (20mg), iron (0.56mg), and vitamin C (9mg) per 100g. Its very low glycemic index (~15) and negligible glycemic load make it ideal for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The near-zero sodium content and good potassium level make it a DASH-friendly choice for hypertension management. Its low saturated fat and cholesterol content support cardiovascular health. Boiled zucchini is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and low-calorie diets, and can be served as a side dish, incorporated into soups, stews, or pasta, or used as a low-carbohydrate substitute for noodles when spiralised.
common_user_queries: is zucchini good for weight loss

confidence_reason: Core macro and micronutrient data are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for the exact food item 'Zucchini, boiled' (cr_id F14040034). Additional micronutrients are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11478), both approved primary sources. GI is estimated from a peer-reviewed journal (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care), which is an approved GI source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for boiled zucchini are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11478) at the level of individual fatty acid classes for this preparation as of 2026. Saturated fat (0.1g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11478) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. Additional micronutrients (vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11478) as a secondary approved source where HPB does not provide these values. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values, Diabetes Care, as no direct entry for boiled zucchini exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Cholesterol is confirmed zero per both HPB and USDA sources. Vitamin B12 and vitamin D are confirmed zero as zucchini is a plant food containing no animal-derived components.

Fixed q4: Changed glycemic_index from null to numeric value 15 (analogous estimate from approved peer-reviewed source) with full gi_source documentation.
